questions.prompt: What does the impossible-travel signal fire on?
choices: The same identity signing in from two locations within a window of time that is physically impossible to cover by travel.

choices.label: A user signing in from outside their employer's home country.
choices.rationale: A single foreign sign-in is not impossible-travel. The signal needs two sign-ins with impossible timing.

choices.label: A user with multiple devices signing in simultaneously.
choices.rationale: Multi-device simultaneous sign-in is normal for most users.

choices.label: A user travelling to a country flagged as high-risk.
choices.rationale: Sanctions lists are not the basis of this signal.

questions.explanation: Geography and timing do not add up. The signal requires two sign-ins from locations that cannot be reached in the elapsed time.

questions.prompt: Which is the strongest marker of a VPN false positive on impossible travel?

choices.label: The user has Outlook on a mobile phone.
choices.rationale: Mobile Outlook alone is not the discriminator.

choices: The anomalous location is a known corporate VPN exit point with months of prior clean sign-ins from the same exit.

choices.label: The user works in IT.
choices.rationale: Role is not the discriminator. Pattern and Timeline are.

choices.label: The user has accepted MFA prompts at unusual hours before.
choices.rationale: That is an MFA-fatigue indicator, not a VPN false-positive marker.

questions.explanation: Long-standing VPN-exit pattern with no follow-on activity is the false-positive shape the SOC weighs.

questions.prompt: Which failure-mode pair does the lesson name?

choices.label: Re-grading every incident, or never re-grading any.
choices.rationale: Re-grading is not the tech's job at all.

choices.label: Always calling the user first and never calling the user.
choices.rationale: User verification is a tool, not the named failure-mode pair.

choices: Treating every impossible-travel signal as Critical.

choices.label: Acting before reading and reading without acting.
choices.rationale: Generic. The specific named pair is over-react vs. dismiss.

questions.explanation: (burns customer relationships) and dismissing them all as 'probably a VPN' (misses real compromise). The discipline is reading the SOC's classification, not pattern-matching your own reaction.

questions.prompt: Which combination of markers is most consistent with true impossible-travel compromise?

choices.label: User has documented international travel, no follow-on activity in the Timeline.
choices.rationale: That is the documented-travel false-positive pattern.

choices.label: Sign-in from a long-standing corporate VPN exit, empty Timeline.
choices.rationale: That is the VPN false-positive pattern.

choices.label: Single sign-in from the user's home country at their normal time.
choices.rationale: No anomaly at all. Would not fire impossible-travel.

choices: Anomalous location with no prior history.
questions.explanation: IP on a residential-proxy list, follow-on inbox-rule creation within minutes. Anomalous location plus malicious IP plus immediate follow-on activity is the true-compromise marker set.

questions.prompt: "VPN exits are always benign." Why is this wrong?

choices.label: VPNs are always malicious.
choices.rationale: Overcorrects in the opposite direction.

choices.label: VPN traffic is invisible to the SOC.
choices.rationale: Sign-in events are visible regardless of VPN use.

choices.label: Huntress cannot see VPN sign-ins.
choices.rationale: Sign-in events appear in the Timeline regardless of network path.

questions.explanation: The SOC's classification weighs both possibilities. Do not blanket-dismiss based on the word 'VPN.' The SOC weighs both benign VPN and attacker VPN. The Recommendation tells you which case this is.

questions.prompt: High-severity impossible-travel on Alex. Bangkok sign-in followed by inbox rule forwarding payment mail and an OAuth grant. Recommendation: full playbook. What do you do?

choices.label: Call Alex first to ask whether he is travelling.
choices.rationale: Wrong sequencing. The Recommendation is the action. The inbox rule is exfiltrating now.

choices.label: Try Alex on his personal phone to verify the travel before acting.
choices.rationale: Same delay cost. The SOC has classified. Act.

questions.explanation: Session revoke, password and MFA reset, inbox-rule removal, OAuth-grant revoke. Document. Reach the manager. Timeline confirms the compromise pattern. Execute per the playbook.

questions.prompt: Impossible travel on Jordan. Only two sign-ins in Timeline, no follow-on. Recommendation: 'Documented travel pattern. Verify with user. No further action if confirmed.' What do you do?
choices: Call Jordan, verify the activity was theirs.

choices.label: Execute the full compromise playbook anyway, to be safe.
choices.rationale: Ignores the SOC's Recommendation. Locks Jordan out for no reason. Damages the customer relationship.

choices.label: Wait for the next incident to confirm a pattern.
choices.rationale: The Recommendation is to verify now. Waiting passes ownership for no benefit.

questions.explanation: Close per the Recommendation if confirmed. The SOC has classified this as the cached-credential / known-travel pattern. Follow the Recommendation.

questions.prompt: Why do IMAP and SMTP clients sometimes fire impossible-travel artefacts on benign users?

choices.label: They run only from anonymising proxies.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the facts.

choices.label: They are forbidden by Huntress's terms of service.
choices.rationale: Not a real platform constraint.

choices.label: They always indicate compromise.
choices.rationale: The lesson names them as a false-positive source.

choices: They cache credentials and can fire impossible-travel-shaped events even for legitimate users.
questions.explanation: IMAP and SMTP credential caching is a known false-positive contributor the SOC weighs in the classification.

questions.prompt: "I can tell whether impossible-travel is real by looking at the IP." Why is this unreliable?

choices.label: IP addresses change too quickly to be useful.
choices.rationale: Not the lesson's framing.

choices: The SOC's classification weighs IP reputation.

choices.label: Tech accounts do not have access to IP-reputation tools.
choices.rationale: Wrong on tooling access.

choices.label: IPs are not part of the Evidence.
choices.rationale: IPs are part of the Evidence. The lesson is about overweighting your own read vs the SOC's full picture.

questions.explanation: User pattern, client type. Follow-on activity together. Tech-seat IP lookups alone are less reliable than the SOC's combined call. The SOC has more context than a single IP lookup. Trust the classification.

questions.prompt: "If it says impossible travel, the user is definitely compromised." Why is this wrong?

choices.label: Impossible travel only fires when the SOC has high confidence.
choices.rationale: Confidence levels vary. The Recommendation tells you the SOC's read.

choices.label: Impossible travel is always benign.
choices.rationale: Overcorrects in the opposite direction.

choices: False-positive rate is real, especially with IMAP/SMTP cached credentials and corporate VPNs.

choices.label: The user can override the impossible-travel signal.
choices.rationale: Users do not override SOC classifications.

questions.explanation: The Recommendation does the discrimination. The signal alone is not the disposition. The Recommendation, informed by VPN patterns, travel history, and follow-on activity, is the disposition.
